I think the nuance here is that the VP you mentioned never coded - so she had no pretense of knowing the technical bits. I would still argue she would have been even better if she learned to code a bit :) 

The examples I'm talking about are more managers that used to code but stopped for years. There knowledge of the work their people are doing is outdated - which might be more dangerous than 'non existing'. 

I think that non critical bug fixes are a great example - why did you feel you were a speedbump/distraction? I also don't place myself in the critical path, but I feel doing such small things in the same environment as my developers (back in my director days too) helps me do my job better.

Greg Stein I think every company is doing it differently, so I'm not 100% sure what exactly you have in mind when you say Scrum team. 

Let's assume an ideal scenario, where you have a strong team/s, with staff/architect, that are both code at architecture and at mentoring newer engineers. 

First of all - this is a rare case - a critical part of our jobs is getting to that situation. Hiring the right people, mentoring those staff/architects, creating team processes that work well and fit our organization. 

In every team, there will be parts that are 'weaker'. For example, maybe your staff/seniors are less product-oriented, and you need to work with the PM more closely. Also dynamics are hard to create - understand who fits where, what projects can you assign to everyone. What about helping engineers get promoted? This is something a staff will rarely help with (otherwise you have an EM on your hands that is just called staff...).

In my opinion, to do all of that - it helps a lot to still understand the job your engineers are doing (especially with all the AI changes)

It’s crazy how you start to notice those things once you have the laws in mind :)

אתה עושה המון, וזה מרשים. אבל אם תמשיך לפעול לרוחב במקום לעומק, אתה תבזבז אנרגיה יקרה ולא תשיג את המקסימום מהמאמצים שלך. יש לך יותר מדי יוזמות פתוחות במקביל – אם לא תכריח את עצמך להגדיר סדרי עדיפויות קיצוניים, אתה תישאר תקוע בתנועה מתמדת אבל בלי התקדמות משמעותית.

צעדים מעשיים וברורים:
1. צמצם פוקוס אגרסיבית – מתוך כל מה שאתה עובד עליו (ניוזלטר, קורסים, אתרים, אפליקציות, ריצות מרתון וכו’), בחר מקסימום שניים שאתה מתחייב להוציא לפועל ברמה יוצאת דופן ב-3 החודשים הקרובים. כל השאר מוקפא או מחוץ לתמונה.

2. מדוד אימפקט, לא מאמץ – עבור כל דבר שאתה עושה, שאל את עצמך: האם זה מניב תוצאה עסקית/אישית משמעותית, או שאני עושה את זה כי זה מעניין/נוח/קל?

3. סגור לופים מהר – אתה נוטה להשאיר דברים פתוחים ולחשוב עליהם יותר מדי. הצב לעצמך דדליין לקבלת החלטות מהירה ולפעולה נחרצת.

The growth (especailly from reocmmendations) is quite addicting on Substack :) 

There are many downsides:
- No segmentation 
- Very poor metrics 
- No a/b tests of titles 

And mainly, the traffic from SEO and other places come to substack. You cannot cross-publish on your personal blog and add it as a canonical URL, Substack doesn't allow that. 

I consider moving from it to my own site multiple times, but couldn't give up on the growth :) 
(and don't believe the 'poor quality of subs' - both from the app and recommendations I get great subscribers). 

I'm actually thinking of a way to combine it - have short and punchy email/article in Substack, with link to the deeper one outside it. Chenell Basilio does it with Growth in Reverse, where you don't get the whole post in the email, just the gist of it.

Few things that come to my mind.

1. A free video course is a big effort. To me, it gives a bit ‘desperate’ vibes, like you don’t value your own content enough. 

2.I know your goal was to reduce it by making it free - but I think I never watched any free course I subscribed to 😅 even the paid ones in Udemy, I have like 10 I bought and didn’t watch/finish. The ones I did finish was ones I paid $100+ for.

3. What is the goal behind it? If it’s to gather email addresses and get them outside LinkedIn, why not a free email course, of 4-6 emails with the content of the course? Or a newsletter, as is more common? Why the metric of 50 people finishing the course, what’s the end game?

4. To get 250 sign ups, you’ll probable need ~10k views on the landing page. At least for my newsletter, the conversion rate is 2-3%. 

5. The best thing that worked for me was to put the link in successful posts, that ‘prove’ I know what I’m talking about. For example if you share a story from Meta/Amazon, adding a CTA in the end.

Wow thanks everyone for all the comments! A short expansion on my take:

What I DON'T mean is EMs who code because it's easier for them than doing the other work, or it's their instinct to solve problems with code. 

I DO think that EVERY manager should code. Yes, also managers of managers. 

As I expect every sales leader to do sales calls, and every customer support leader to talk to customers. 

The trick is that it doesn't have to take that long. While I mentioned a few hours a week, it can be even a few hours a year!  

The biggest excuse I've read in the comments it that 'there are more important tasks for a manager than coding' - and I agree. But FOR SURE you can find a day once a year for taking a small bug on yourself. That's all I'm advocating for. 

Doing PRs is great, but it's not a substitute for doing a full task yourself. It'll help you learn what your developers our going through, the current state of the IDE (and coding LLMs), the CI/CD process, your documentation, and many more useful lessons!

Then, we got much more transparent. Nobody was surprised when the second layoffs wave came. The good part is both the people laid off took it better, as they understood the reasoning, and those who stayed.

If I take the overused ship analogy - instead of looking to jump ships, people tried to help their ship to not sink, because they felt a part of it.

Sima the question is which comes first? I’ve worked with amazing PMs, where I was consulted early in the process, I felt a part of the team, and I never put sticks in the wheels.

I’ve also worked in an environment where the PMs didn’t do any discovery, accepted the roadmap from the executive team, and was content with the project management part.

So in my experience, the better the PM do their job, the less friction there is. 

I feel it’s part of my responsibility to make sure we are working on the right things, and ask the hard questions.

Sima you have a difficult job :) 

My best tip is to over-share the reasoning, and never be satisfied with a meeting that ends with “that’s my decision”. I know it’s frustrating, but as the EMs are not direct reports, that approach just doesn’t work.

And even if it’s more subtle, if you have even a small inclining that the EM is not happy with the decision, take the time to go through it in a 1:1. Share customer feedback, invite people on sessions, share your thought process.

Personally, I hate to be treated as a mercenary. I feel the PM job is critical, but I feel that in many cases I can do the job better. I know this sounds awful and overconfident, but I think that’s the hidden reality in many interactions, so it’s important to discuss that.

I may be a bit delusional, but I did perform both jobs for 8 months, and I’ve read all of Marty Cagan’s books (especially liked the last one, Transformed), and I don’t think I’m the only such EM. 

So to sum it up - it’s very important for us to know you are steering the ship in the right direction.

In my opinion, the PMs will SPECIALIZE in the industry and product type. Aatir Abdul Rauf had a great take on it:

There's a growing trend of specialization in Product Management. The advice I’ve been contemplating on is “1 Major, 2 Minors”:

1. Major in product management.
Product Management is no joke and it takes time to master. Ensure you develop strong foundations in discovery, strategy, roadmaps, prioritization, stakeholder management, and product analytics. Without that, you’ll struggle no matter what the domain.

2. Minor in a product type.
Pick up a product type like B2B and get depth in how the buying cycle works and the considerations PMs have to make in it.

3. Minor in an industry.
Take time to build depth in domain knowledge and get into the weeds of it.

This might seem like an awful lot but a solid 2-3 year stint at a growing company can enable you to achieve this.

Paweł Huryn 🇺🇦 I would say in general - a developer was promoted to a management role, and you are the PM working with him/her. There are many aspects of that relationship - you'll need to help create the boundaries between you, and cover for mistakes, for example. 

On the other hand, if you start working with a team was a super experienced EM, that has his own agenda and way of doing things. That presents a different challenge for you, as you'll probably get more pushback.

There are other 'archetypes' of EMs, each requiring a different adjustment from the PM. The one that is deep in the code and don't care about the business and always pushes for tech debt, the one that wants to join every customer call and make decisions on the roadmap, and so on.

📌Addressing the comments I got about how my post is hateful, sad, and I should be sued for it:

IT'S NOT ABOUT AGE. Junior/Senior are titles. I made fun of seniors, as being slow and taking the 'convential' path (a ladder vs a trampoline). You can be a 50-year-old junior. I've been working for 2 years with a freelancer who is over 60 (I don't know exactly, because I don't care!), and I love working with him. It's not about having a bunch of 20-year-olds only.

Regarding salary, complaining, and how I promote 'slavery'. 

Juniors are willing to do things that a more experienced engineer might feel overqualified for. We are not talking about factories here, even the 'hard work' is creating software!

Seniors in Tel Aviv make >$130K a year. An amazing salary on all counts. Juniors $70-75K. I don't consider it 'cheap' workforce.

When the times are tough, you want to maximize your budget. And when you want to hire someone from your team - there is a higher chance you'll get approval for a junior, due to the lower salary. 

I cannot control how people interpret my message. I will definitely improve for the next posts, but I feel that 90% got my message and 10% chose to be offended.

Barbara I understand your point, but I don't agree with you. 

Your translations are your interpretations, not something I said. Yes, juniors complain less, and are willing to do things that a more experienced engineer might feel overqualified for (like more manual work, or working on patching up old systems). The are less picky about the work they do. 

Regarding the 7th point - seniors in Tel Aviv make at least $130K a year. An amazing salary on all counts. Juniors can agree for $70-75K. I don't consider it 'cheap' workforce, and there is not intention in draining people. 

When your organization is in a hiring freeze, or even worth - firing, you want to maximize your budget. That's life. And when you want to hire someone from your team - there is a higher chance you'll get approval for a junior, due to the lower salary.

I cannot control how people interpret what I mean. I will definitely improve for the next posts, but I feel that 90% got my message and 10% CHOSE to be offended.

Paulo André that’s a discussion I often have with my manager. He feels that it’s useful to have people of all kinds - because those rarely bored people can help you save the good and stressful stuff to the ones who want it.

Nicely put. The hard part, is that this channel is both in different locations for each people, and in different 'width'. 

1. Rarely bored and rarely stressed - the 'easiest' people to manage, with the widest channel. They are satisfied with what they do. 
2. Rarely bored and easily stressed - the people most need your help, to feel confident to get outside their comfort zone
3. Easily bored and easily stressed - this one is very difficult for me. Those people usually complain a lot, and it's very hard to keep them satisfied. Because it frustrates us, we tend to 'give up', or try to make them less bored in boring tasks. Instead, we should help them to utilize their abilities, increasing the anxiety thershold. 
4.  easily bored and rarely stressed - promote those 🙃

Reading 'Radical Candor' a couple of years ago really opened my eyes. I was stuck at Ruinous empathy without even knowing it. 

It's a 2 layer escape:
- First, we convince ourselves that it's best for the other person. We think we don't do that because we don't want to 'hurt' the other side. 
- When we understand that's a lie, we convince ourselves that we will lose the 'love' of the other person. We think the reaction will be awful and the relationship will be ruined. 

For me - it doesn't get easier with time. Each time I need to have a tough conversation, I need to remind myself why shouldn't I avoid it. 

By it's worth it, every time.

Estimating 'known unknowns', the surprises we are used to, also gets better with time.

Imagine a group of developers working on the Uber app for a few years. How accurate would their estimations for a new feature be? My guess is pretty accurate.

From my experience, the problem is not that bad 😅 Not bragging here - in the past couple of years I was able to complete all the projects on time, and on scope. I feel that the main reason is not my awesome estimation skills, but the smaller scope of the projects. 

I would say that most team leaders can estimate a sprint's scope with relative accuracy. So if a project is scoped at no more than 2 sprints, don't you think there is a much higher chance of the estimation to be correct? Especially at startups, it's anyway much better to start with small MVPs. 

I think the other factor is the amount of expertise - projects in areas you are highly familiar with tend to be estimated much more accurately, almost 'negating' the 90-90 rule.

From my experience, you can get a general feel in surveys, but nothing really practical got a specific person.

I try to gather direct feedback from other people about things not directly related to them.

I've seen two main cases with retros-gone-wrong:
1. People are raising the same issues over and over again. When people feel that the retros have no effect, they'll stop raising issues. 

2. There are TOO MANY issues. The TL has months of work ahead to just improve the takeaways from previous retros. 

In other cases, with a focused improvement of the takeaways - I think they can be quite useful.

James Maybe I'm biased because of my DevOps past :) 

In an ideal world, I agree. I still think we are far from it. Developers need to understand and write docker files, they need to understand memory/CPU utilization (even if they use FaaS) and so on. 

The 'DevOps' part of a developer job should be as low as possible. Buy for now, I think it should still exist.

Shai In my experience, the DevOps teams love for the engineers to be involved. 

There is a middle ground between just knowing the best practices and taking ownership - seeing it as a mutual effort. 

I learned a lot from Michael Zion's approach toward it, from the DevOps side. The closer they are with the developers - the better the outcome for the company.

Jordan I think it's crucial. Developers tend to 'wash their hands' of the task once it's blocked. Somehow, it's not their responsibility anymore to deliver it on time.

To promptly unblock a task, someone still needs to own it and push forward.

Bad ❌ -> I'm blocked on X, so I'm waiting.
Good ✅ -> I'm blocked on X, so I started progress on Y.
Best 🌟  -> I'm blocked on X. I talked to Z about it, and I'll make sure to speed up the unblocking. Meanwhile, I started progress on Y.

Jordan Kudos for the effort 😅

We had a discussion about our daily in the last team retreat. It had grown from 10-15 minutes, to 20-25, as the team grew. In our case, ALL of the developers wanted it left as it is - not reducing the cadence, or making it more ‘updates’ focused.

The main benefit the cited is they enjoy seeing what others work on, hear about interested problems, and be kept in the loop. It always is to switch tasks much more easily, and when someone come to help - they have more context.

🕸 Daniel Aranias נכון, אני אישית משער שהוא החסם הכי נפוץ בפרויקטי צד שמנסים לבנות בפומבי

סימנתי את התשובה הכי קרובה - בסוף היום אין לי כוח לכלום.
זה לא לגמרי מדויק - לקח לי שנים להתחיל לכתוב בפומבי (למרות שמאוד רציתי) בגלל אוסף של חסמי כניסה:

1. הייתי צריך הרבה אנרגיה נפשית להתחלה, והמסגרות המלאות לקחו לי את רובה. בהתחלה זה היה התואר תוך כדי משרה מלאה, ואז זה היה תפקיד ראש הצוות שהתאבדתי עליו. רק לפני חצי שנה, אחרי שנה כראש צוות, נהיה לי הפנאי ה'שכלי' להתעסק. זה לפחות מה שאני משער, ולכן סימנתי את התשובה הזו. 

2. הפחד של מה אנשים יגידו, במיוחד אם הפרויקט הוא תוכן לאנשים אחרים. אנשים קרובים מסביבי מגיבים שזה די פתטי, ולא מבינים את הפואנטה, כמו שציפיתי שיקרה. לקח לי זמן להבין איך להתעלם מהקולות האלה בראש שלי. 
גם עם פרויקט פיתוח, התחושה שאנשים מסביבך יגידו לך שזה מטומטם. 

3. המסגרת גם חשובה. בהתחלה עשיתי איזה פוסט והפסקתי לחודש. ואז עשיתי עוד אחד והפסקתי לשבועיים. לקחו לי כמה חודשים להיכנס לקצב, להבין מה מתאים לי, ולמצוא בדיוק את ההרגלים האטומיים (ספר מדהים) ששרדו תקופות קשות.

Gregor Ojstersek now I got your point. I agree about different type of changes - there should be a clear focus. The coomunication of the gradual change is also a good solution.

But often changes have to be surprising, and big. Let’s take layoffs. In Facebook, they did layoffs in multiple parts - which was a huge hit for morale (based on what my friend in Facebook say). Even though Zuckerberg said they will be more layoffs in X months.

Personally, small changes may feel like trying to slowly rip the bandage.. it can be more painful then just ripping it off at once 😅

If there are multiple changes, it can create a feeling that the management doesn’t know what they are doing. If it’s obvious in hindsight the some of the changes could have been made before, it can also affect the trust.

In some cases it may be suitable,  but in my opinion it’s the minority of cases.

I think that not always you need to go back to fix the debt. 

For example - we have an old project which is still in use. It’s VERY hard to maintain, no UTs, old versions and so on. 

There was a request for a small feature there. During implementation, we mapped out the debts, broke it down to chunks and estimated.

And then we left it as it is. We said to the product team, that if we’ll need to write a big feature in that area, we’ll have to tackle X and Y first, and this is the estimation. 

That was 2 years ago, and we haven’t touched it since…

The differentiation between operations/product teams is important, but I think it's not clear-cut. I agree with the generalization - Kanban is more suitable in most cases. But I think it really depends on the personalities. The more senior/responsible the operation team is, the better Kanban will work.

In less mature/responsible teams, the removal of the scrum 'pressure' can have negative effects, with tasks dragging on forever. When it's easy to push things to the top of your board, the important and long-term stuff might never get done.

So when choosing Kanban, it's important to have deadlines in addition. 

Regarding the Product team's process, the 'classic' scrum - great summary. My only addition is a meeting we have with is 'R&D grooming' for 15 minutes every sprint to promote R&D-wide initiatives. It's similar to the backlog grooming, just with Architects/team-leads.

I think my main mistake was being too ‘distruptive’, not trying hard enough to engage with Boris and your team. I was convinced that my way is better and alienated people.

So I would advice to try much harder to explain your reasoning and actions to the people already there. Consult with them, and when you take a different decision - make sure to show that you respect the other side, and why you took that decision.

Here's the CTE with Rank and Partition: 

WITH monarch_ranks AS (
    SELECT 
        name, 
        reign_start_year, 
        reign_end_year, 
        country,
        RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY country ORDER BY reign_start_year) as rank
    FROM 
        monarchs 
    WHERE 
        country IN ('Britain', 'Belgium')
)

SELECT *
FROM monarch_ranks
WHERE rank = 3;

In short - I’m against it :)
But that’s just a personal preference. I think that once every 3 weeks is too far away though.

I think that a weekly meeting gives a sense of ‘continuation’, it keeps you involved. In addition, When the cadence is lower, it’s easier to move them around or ‘skip’ a week. Once in 3 can become one a month, etc.

If you want a longer meeting, you can do it too - let’s say every month to have 1 hour or 90 minute meetings. I agree sometimes it provides a deeper conversation.

I think the first one is tricky. I would even say I don’t agree with that.
The whole point, of building the connection, is consistency. As Vladimir mentioned here - for some people it will not be natural at start.

If we cancel a meeting, Usually it’s not because we don’t want to ‘force it’, but because it’s easier for us. It’s with the people we feel awkward talking too.

I believe that any pair of 2 people have things to talk about for 30 minutes a week, even without agenda. Often I don’t prepare anything and neither they, and I never cancel.

Loved the last one! I was surprised how often people don’t ask it of themselves…

And the second tip is also golden. It’s how I felt at the beginning, that “they just don’t want to talk, so it’s ok to skip it”. Bad trap.

I think that even once is too often :)
You might move it, but canceling without a good reason immediately sets a bad precedent.

- It's much more complex than REST. A simple example - inside your message, you have an integer value. by default, it will receive the value 0. How do you differentiate if you received 0 from the request, or you didn't receive anything? There is no standard and clear solution, and it creates havoc (same with booleans).

- There is a big learning curve. Developers need to adjust. Especially full stack ones, who are used to quickly working with a backend. 

- Creating UT and mocks for it is harder. 

- The build process for NodeJS client packages was hard to build. There were different libraries, and it took some time to make it work correctly.

- Postman didn't support it (until less than a year ago). It's very hard to teach our support team to use some endpoints by themselves.

That's a partial list :)

And my biggest concern - we don't really utilize the performance benefits it brings. We have much bigger bottlenecks.

When I finished the text, I felt some resistance - as a I'm VERY into quick and dirty, and I'm not a big fan of processes in general :)

Those steps, are the processes BEFORE the development. They save you lots of money, mistakes, and heartache. 

But still - there is a place for quick and dirty. Sometimes you can't get the processes in place. You have to deliver something, and then build on top of it (the 'agile' way).
Love it. It gets really confusing in 'authoritative' organizations, like the army. You get much more authority, and sometimes forget to make the choice of leadership.

It becomes even more difficult in management roles, where more pieces need to move to accommodate a promotion. 

Great advice by Jordan. I would summarize it like this:
1. Prove you are ready for a promotion, by giving everything you have. 
2. Have a direct conversation, where get feedback from your manager, and discuss the possibilities and plans for promotion.
3. Circle back in 3 months. If you didn't get a response, mentioned kindly, that you feel you are ready for a promotion in the near future. If it won't workout in this company, you will probably look for somewhere else - no hard feelings.

I've actually done the last part. It's not a 'hard ultimatum'. You don't have to say 'it's either a promotion or I'm out!'. You clearly outline that you feel you outgrew your current role, and if the company has no vacancies, it's ok, but you'll look elsewhere.

Ziv I tried to average it out - I agree that some fresh employees come with low motivation. In my opinion, we should make the expectations really quickly, really clear. 
If they stay 'tired' - they should go.

They are also exceptions the other way - lots of experience, and lots of caring and motivation.

But as a general rule, I think it is a slow decline. It's hard to avoid that, you are just less moved, because you already saw a lot of things.

I feel the 'Requirements are not ready' is a symptom of a bigger problem, that I would have called an 'ineffective discovery process'.

We can be at 100% every sprint, but still not release on time. If the original estimation of a big project was 3 months, and each sprint we discovered new things we need to do, it can take 6, while the engineers always feel like they finish on time.

As you mentioned, it can be helped by involving the engineers during the initial phases, but that's not enough. The requirements can still change, if it was not done in a way that involves ALL stake holders in the company.

Personally, I’m not a fun of big refactors. It takes couple of days, but usually, even old code can be understood and improved.

I loved the part of not being surprised - if you are, it definitely means something is wrong with the communication between you and your manager. 

It happened to me both ways - as an employee (I was very surprised at a bad review), and as a manager. 

The first tip, which seems obvious to you - is to take the process seriously. We usually have a basic template, and all the employees like to do the minimum to fill it out. It's especially true if you do a GOOD job during the 6 month period, as they know they won't be surprised, so they feel less need to prepare. 

It's important to understand how great an opportunity it is, both to reflect on work well done, and to show yourself to the higher level-ups.

There is a fine line, between blaming and ignoring.

I like to divide the mistakes/broken things into 2 types:
1. An honest mistake:
- You missed a test case.
- You forgot an important step, because you wanted to release ASAP. 
- You pressed the wrong button by mistake.

Those types of mistakes should be easily forgiven. We should learn from them for sure - not just ignore them. Understand why they happened, and how can we minimize them happening again.

2. Careless behaviour, such as:
- Directly editing a DB when you have an endpoint.
- Disobeying a clear rule to save a few minutes (like not testing in QA first). 

This type of mistake, I think we can accept only once. The second time, the developer should definitely be held responsible. There should be a standard of working, otherwise your application will break every other day.

But often, what is clear to us, valid reasons for cutting code or completely changing features, is less clear for the developers.

Putting that aside - when revisiting older code, we should definetly trim it mercifully.

One of my main takeaways, is that Ruinous Empathy can be much worse even than Obnoxious aggression. 

It's very common for 'nice' people to fall into that ruinous empathy trap. Sparing people's feelings and not giving hard feedback (I'm one of those).

I'm trying to remind myself, that withholding the feedback - is just because I want to spare myself a difficult conversation. And that's a sign of a weak leader.

I think that being blocked and stuck are 2 different things, that require a different approach.

Jordan Cutler Covered well the part about being blocked.

Being stuck, with a problem you need to solve but can't, is harder to overcome. 

My guidelines are usually:
- The closer you are to the deadline, the more the scale should tip towards asking for help.
- Try to imagine what a more senior engineer (the one you are going to ask for help) will ask, or do. Don't be lazy and assume he'll do it for you.
- When you do ask for help, make sure to provide all the things you tried, and the context.
- NEVER transfer the ownership of the task. Even if someone dives deep to help you, make sure to understand every step in the solution.

It can be unconscious - you might delay hard decisions because you know you will be there in 3 months.

Itzy Sabo But how to tackle the pairings?

If we do it for developers at the same level, the less experienced ones have less to learn from each other.

Dalibor Plavcic wow, that's actually really useful! 

It addresses the whole issue in the post in the 'speed' part. Loved this paragraph:

How Fast Should Code Reviews Be?
If you are not in the middle of a focused task, you should do a code review shortly after it comes in.

One business day is the maximum time it should take to respond to a code review request (i.e., first thing the next morning).

Following these guidelines means that a typical CL should get multiple rounds of review (if needed) within a single day.

Itzy Sabo I'm almost always against refactor, so can't say I'm balanced 😂

Love this topic. It's an often overlooked one. 
The second one is the most important in my opinion. Often, the team leader might miss the bigger picture, or get carried away. If each develop asks themselves what are the goals, what is the highest priority, it can change the whole sprint planning.

Same for the first one - it's our job to give rejects to the product team.

The always-refactoring mindset has it costs. 

Let's take for example (based on my experience, true story) an area that is not very prominent in the roadmap. It's an important product, but it's ok in how it works. Small changes now and then are required. 

The code base is pretty horrible. It was written very fast, in the younger days of the company. To do a good refactor, will take couple of months (you can't really do it in a very small chunk, as you need to touch APIs to do it properly).

My take, is NOT to refactor it at all. Patch-on-patch. Might be, this product will be deprecated before we ever need to refactor -> lots of time saved.

I don't think the point of the PRs is to be a 'safeguard'.
In my opinion, they serve 3 purposes:
* Getting developers to align on company standards (design patterns, readability, etc), to make sure the code base has some similarity. 
* Raising questions/issues the developer might have missed, side-effects of the code, etc. 
* Teaching junior developers

We hired 4 developers in Ukraine 2 years ago, and without 3/4, it didn't work out. They had different values - they wanted a lot of freedom, very flexible working hours, and the delivery pace was much slower.

Because of cultural differences, it's also hard to judge and interview for the right things.

- A CTO can fix a bug

The most similar thing to Starbucks' CEO serving coffee (and making his execs do the same).
